text:
THORACIC ONCOLOGY CLINIC -- RETURN VISIT
ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE (CLINICAL TRIAL PROTOCOL VISIT)

PATIENT: Margaret Yuen
AGE: 64 years
DIAGNOSIS: Stage IIIB (T3N3M0) non-small cell lung cancer, adenocarcinoma, left upper lobe; EGFR L858R/T790M, MET-amplified, STK11/KEAP1 co-mutant
CURRENT REGIMEN: Tepotinib 500 mg PO daily + osimertinib (Tagrisso) 80 mg PO daily (clinical trial, approximately cycle 4, 4 months on study)
ATTENDING: [Attending Oncologist]
CLINICAL TRIAL ID: NCT[REDACTED] -- Phase I/II tepotinib + osimertinib in EGFR-mutant NSCLC with MET amplification post-EGFR TKI

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Ms. Yuen presents for protocol-specified clinical trial follow-up visit. She is here for evaluation of tolerability, toxicity assessment, and review of recent restaging imaging.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:

Ms. Yuen is a 64-year-old Chinese-American woman with a well-characterized history of Stage IIIB left upper lobe lung adenocarcinoma, initially diagnosed two years ago. Her tumor harbored an EGFR L858R point mutation (exon 21), with co-mutations in STK11, KEAP1, and TP53, and baseline low-level MET amplification. She was treated with definitive concurrent chemoradiation (carboplatin/paclitaxel weekly x6 weeks with 60 Gy in 30 fractions via IMRT to the left upper lobe primary and mediastinal stations 4R, 4L, 7, and left hilum), followed by consolidation/maintenance osimertinib 80 mg daily, per the LAURA trial paradigm. She achieved a good partial response on Tagrisso, with the primary lesion shrinking from 3.8 cm down to approximately 1.3 cm at best response around 9 months.

Unfortunately, at approximately 14 months on osimertinib, she developed systemic and intracranial progression. Rebiopsy of a new right lower lobe nodule confirmed retained adenocarcinoma histology (no small cell transformation), with emergence of an EGFR T790M resistance mutation and high-level MET amplification (copy number ratio 5.8, up from 2.4 at diagnosis) as co-resistance mechanisms on repeat NGS. A PIK3CA E545K mutation was also newly identified. Brain MRI at that time revealed two new parenchymal metastases: a 1.4 cm right frontal lesion with modest vasogenic edema and a 0.7 cm left cerebellar lesion.

She subsequently underwent SRS to both brain metastases (right frontal 20 Gy x1, left cerebellar 22 Gy x1) with excellent radiographic response -- the cerebellar lesion is no longer visible and the right frontal site has evolved to a small necrotic remnant. Systemically, she completed 6 cycles of carboplatin AUC 5 / pemetrexed (Alimta) 500 mg/m2 q21d, achieving stable disease/minor partial response, followed by a brief bridge with single-agent pemetrexed maintenance. She was then enrolled on the current Phase I/II clinical trial evaluating tepotinib (MET inhibitor) 500 mg daily in combination with osimertinib 80 mg daily, targeting both the T790M resistance mechanism and the MET amplification bypass pathway.

She is currently approximately 4 months (cycle 4) into trial therapy. At the 8-week on-study CT restaging, she demonstrated a meaningful partial response: the left upper lobe lesion decreased from 1.8 x 1.4 cm to 1.2 x 0.9 cm, the right lower lobe nodule resolved, and the right adrenal nodule resolved. Six-month post-SRS brain MRI showed no new CNS lesions and continued post-treatment evolution of the right frontal site.

Today she reports that overall she is doing well. She notes continued mild bilateral ankle swelling, which she attributes to the tepotinib. She is managing this with compression stockings and watching her sodium intake -- she thinks the swelling has been stable and not worsening over the past several weeks. She reports occasional loose stools (one to two episodes per day, sometimes three), which she manages with dietary modification and occasional loperamide; this has been stable and tolerable. She denies nausea, vomiting, or abdominal cramping. She denies dyspnea, hemoptysis, chest pain, palpitations, or new cough. She specifically denies headaches, visual changes, word-finding difficulty, focal weakness, sensory changes, or new neurological symptoms. She notes her energy level has improved considerably compared to when she was on the platinum doublet -- she has resumed her morning walks (30 minutes daily) and has been doing some gardening, which she finds meaningful. Appetite is good. She is taking all medications as directed with no missed doses. She denies any falls.

She asks today about how long she might remain on the trial and about the significance of her tumor marker trend.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: Mild fatigue, improved from prior; no fever, chills, or night sweats; weight stable
- Pulmonary: Mild exertional breathlessness on vigorous effort, unchanged from baseline post-CRT fibrosis; no new dyspnea at rest or with ordinary activity; no hemoptysis; occasional dry cough (chronic, post-radiation)
- Cardiovascular: Bilateral lower extremity edema as above; no palpitations, chest pain, or orthopnea
- GI: Grade 1 diarrhea as above; no nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, blood in stool
- Neurological: Denies headache, visual symptoms, dizziness, ataxia, focal weakness, numbness
- Skin: Persistent mild acneiform eruption on face, chest, and upper back; no skin breakdown or infection
- Musculoskeletal: No new bone pain or back pain
- Hematologic: No unusual bruising or bleeding
- Endocrine/metabolic: No lower extremity pain (ruling out DVT in the setting of edema)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:

General: Alert, pleasant woman in no acute distress. Well-groomed, comfortable in the exam room. Speaking in full sentences.

Vital signs: Blood pressure 124/76 mmHg, heart rate 74 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16, temperature 36.8 C, oxygen saturation 96% on room air. Weight: [per nursing note]. BMI: within normal limits.

HEENT: No scleral icterus. Pupils equal and reactive. Oropharynx clear. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy appreciated.

Skin: Grade 1 acneiform papulo-pustular eruption on bilateral cheeks, nasal bridge, anterior chest, and upper back. No vesicles, no desquamation, no superinfection. No palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia.

Pulmonary: Diminished breath sounds in the left upper zone consistent with known post-radiation fibrosis and prior treatment. No wheezing, rhonchi, or new focal changes. No pleural friction rub.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs. JVP not elevated.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly. No palpable masses.

Extremities: 1+ pitting edema bilateral ankles, symmetric, non-tender, without erythema or warmth. No calf tenderness. Peripheral pulses intact.

Neurological: Alert and oriented x4. Cranial nerves II-XII intact. Strength 5/5 in all extremities. Gait steady. Finger-nose-finger coordination intact. No pronator drift.

Lymph nodes: No axillary, inguinal, or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (obtained today, per protocol):

CBC: WBC 6.2 x10^9/L, hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL (mild anemia, improved from nadir of 9.1 g/dL on platinum doublet), platelets 198 x10^9/L
Comprehensive metabolic panel: Sodium 139, potassium 4.1, creatinine 0.82 mg/dL (CrCl estimated ~78 mL/min, stable), BUN 14, total bilirubin 0.7, AST 28 (ULN 40), ALT 32 (ULN 45), alkaline phosphatase 74, albumin 3.9
LFTs within normal limits -- no hepatotoxicity signal
PT/INR: 1.1 (not on anticoagulation)
Magnesium: 1.9 mg/dL (low-normal; will supplement)
ECG (obtained per protocol): Normal sinus rhythm, QTc 432 ms (within acceptable range, no concerning prolongation)
CEA: 8 ng/mL (trending down from a peak of 42 ng/mL at time of progression on osimertinib; prior value at 8-week on-trial scan was ~12 ng/mL; prior to trial initiation was ~19 ng/mL)

---

IMAGING REVIEW:

Most recent CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (approximately 8 weeks ago, on-trial cycle 2 restaging): Left upper lobe residual lesion 1.2 x 0.9 cm, decreased from 1.8 x 1.4 cm. Right lower lobe nodule resolved. Right adrenal nodule resolved. No new intrathoracic or extrathoracic metastatic disease. Findings represent a confirmed partial response to tepotinib + osimertinib.

Most recent brain MRI (approximately 6 weeks ago, 6-month post-SRS follow-up): Right frontal SRS site shows progressive reduction with minimal rim enhancement, 0.5 cm, consistent with ongoing post-treatment evolution or radiation necrosis; no evidence of viable active metastasis. Left cerebellar SRS site: complete radiographic response, no residual lesion visible. No new intracranial metastatic lesions. No leptomeningeal disease.

Next on-trial CT restaging: scheduled in approximately 6 weeks (cycle 6, 6-month on-trial scan).
Next brain MRI: scheduled per protocol at 1-year post-SRS mark.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. Stage IIIB NSCLC, adenocarcinoma, EGFR L858R/T790M, MET-amplified -- currently on Phase I/II trial with tepotinib + osimertinib, approximately 4 months on study:
   - Ms. Yuen is doing well on trial. She has a confirmed partial response systemically with ongoing intracranial control, which is a very encouraging early signal from this combination strategy.
   - The CEA trend is gratifying -- dropping from 42 at nadir of progression to 8 ng/mL currently, still above the reference upper limit but clearly moving in the right direction. I reviewed this with her and explained that CEA is an imperfect biomarker but is being followed as a supplementary indicator; imaging remains the primary assessment tool.
   - Continue tepotinib 500 mg PO daily and osimertinib 80 mg PO daily. No dose modifications at this time.
   - Repeat CT chest/abdomen/pelvis in approximately 6 weeks per protocol schedule.
   - Brain MRI at 1-year post-SRS milestone per trial protocol and standard surveillance.
   - Continue folic acid 400 mcg daily (held over from pemetrexed era; will reassess necessity at next visit) and vitamin B12 supplementation.

2. Tepotinib-related peripheral edema (Grade 1, bilateral ankles):
   - Stable, not worsening. This is a well-recognized class effect of MET inhibitors. No functional impairment.
   - Continue compression stockings (20-30 mmHg gradient), low-sodium diet counseling reinforced.
   - BMP reviewed -- no hyponatremia, no hypoalbuminemia, creatinine stable. No indication for diuresis at this time.
   - Will monitor; if edema worsens to Grade 2 with functional impairment, will consider low-dose furosemide and reassess dose modification per trial protocol.
   - Bilateral lower extremity Doppler not indicated at this time given no asymmetry, erythema, warmth, or calf tenderness; clinical picture most consistent with medication-related peripheral edema.

3. Osimertinib-related diarrhea (Grade 1):
   - Unchanged, manageable with dietary modification and intermittent loperamide. No Grade 2 or higher diarrhea. No dehydration.
   - Reinforce dietary strategies: low-fiber foods, BRAT-type modifications when symptomatic, adequate oral hydration.
   - No dose modification needed at this time.

4. Acneiform rash (Grade 1, osimertinib-related):
   - Mild and cosmetically manageable. Topical clindamycin 1% solution applied to affected areas twice daily. Gentle non-comedogenic moisturizer recommended.
   - No oral antibiotics needed currently. No dose modification.

5. Mild anemia (hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL):
   - Improved significantly from prior nadir on platinum chemotherapy. Stable from prior check. Likely residual from prior cytotoxic therapy; no active myelosuppression anticipated from current trial regimen.
   - Iron studies pending (ordered today). ESA therapy discontinued when platinum was stopped; no current indication to restart.
   - Follow at next visit.

6. Hypomagnesemia (Mg 1.9 mg/dL):
   - Low-normal, asymptomatic. Start magnesium oxide 400 mg PO twice daily. Will recheck at next visit.

7. Intracranial disease -- post-SRS surveillance:
   - Outstanding response to SRS. Both treated sites showing excellent radiographic response. No new CNS disease on most recent MRI.
   - Continue surveillance per protocol; next brain MRI at 1-year post-SRS time point.
   - Patient counseled to report any new neurological symptoms immediately.

8. Goals of care / patient education:
   - I spent time with Ms. Yuen discussing the significance of her response to trial therapy. I explained that the combination of tepotinib targeting the MET amplification and osimertinib addressing the T790M resistance mechanism represents a rational, biologically informed approach, and that the early response data from this trial and similar strategies are genuinely encouraging.
   - She asked how long she might remain on trial. I explained that we will continue as long as she continues to benefit and tolerates treatment, and that the trial does not have a predefined maximum duration for responders.
   - She expressed ongoing interest in continuing trial participation and maintaining her quality of life. Her goals remain disease control and preservation of function, and she is achieving both at this visit.
   - Caregiver support: she reports a supportive family network. No social work or palliative care referral needed at this time; she is well-connected to supportive resources and her functional status is good.

RETURN VISIT: Approximately 6 weeks for next protocol-specified CT restaging and clinical assessment. She is instructed to call the clinic triage line or trial coordinator if she develops any new or worsening symptoms, fever, neurological changes, or edema worsening prior to next scheduled visit.

ECOG Performance Status: 1
Cancer Status: Present, partial response on current clinical trial regimen

[Dictated and signed by Attending Oncologist]
[Fellow co-signature pending]
